title: Low level design for Splitwise (expense sharing). Class diagram, patterns, narrated code that runs, and what to say.

transcript.text: Low level design for Splitwise (expense sharing). Class diagram, patterns, narrated code that runs, and what to say.

blocks.md:
# Splitwise — Low Level Design

## 1. Requirements & Clarifications

**Problem restated:** Build the core of an expense-sharing app. A user pays for something, splits the cost among a group of people (equally, by exact amounts, or by percentage), and the system tracks **who owes whom** so balances can be queried and settled.

**Functional requirements (in scope):**
- Register users; create groups.
- Add an expense: a payer, a total amount, and a split across participants.
- Support split types: **EQUAL**, **EXACT**, **PERCENT**.
- Maintain running **net balances** between every pair of users.
- Query "what do I owe / who owes me".
- **Settle up** (record a repayment that reduces a balance).

**Clarifying questions I'd ask the interviewer:**
- Single currency or multi-currency? *(Assume single currency for now; money as `Decimal`.)*
- Do we need **debt simplification** (minimize number of transactions across a group)? *(I'll model balances so it's a clean add-on; implement pairwise net.)*
- In-memory or persisted? Single process or distributed? *(In-memory, single process; I'll call out the DB/locking boundary.)*
- Can an expense include the payer as a participant? *(Yes — payer often consumes part of it.)*
- Partial settlements allowed? *(Yes.)*

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** single currency, in-memory, thread-safe single process, money as `Decimal` with cent-level rounding where the split has a remainder, and the invariant that **split shares always sum exactly to the expense total**.

> 💬 "Before I design, let me lock scope: I'll support equal/exact/percent splits, track pairwise net balances, and allow settlement. I'll treat money as decimal and keep it single-currency and in-memory. The interesting parts are the split strategies and keeping balances consistent under concurrency — I'll focus there."

---

## 2. Use Cases & Actors

**Actors:**
- **User** — pays for expenses, owes/gets owed, settles up.
- **System (ExpenseManager)** — validates, computes splits, updates balances.

**Core use cases:**
- Register a user.
- Add an expense with a chosen split type → balances update.
- View my balances (who I owe, who owes me).
- Settle up with another user (full or partial).
- (Edge) Reject invalid expenses — bad percentages, exact amounts that don't sum, unknown users.

---

## 3. Core Entities (Objects) & Their Data

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) |
|---|---|---|
| `User` | Identity of a participant | `user_id: str`, `name: str`, `email: str` |
| `Split` | One participant's owed share of an expense | `user: User`, `amount: Decimal` |
| `Expense` | An immutable record of a payment + how it splits | `expense_id: str`, `description: str`, `amount: Decimal`, `paid_by: User`, `splits: list[Split]`, `split_type: SplitType` |
| `SplitStrategy` (abstract) | Validate inputs + compute each participant's share | (stateless) |
| `SplitStrategyFactory` | Map a `SplitType` to its strategy | `_registry: dict[SplitType, SplitStrategy]` |
| `BalanceSheet` | The source of truth for pairwise net debt | `_balances: dict[str, dict[str, Decimal]]` |
| `ExpenseManager` | Orchestrator/facade; owns users, expenses, balances, lock | `_users`, `_expenses`, `_sheet: BalanceSheet`, `_lock: RLock` |

**Enum:** `SplitType { EQUAL, EXACT, PERCENT }`.

**Invariants guarded:**
- `Expense`: `sum(split.amount for split in splits) == amount` (shares reconcile to total, to the cent).
- `BalanceSheet`: antisymmetry — `balances[A][B] == -balances[B][A]` always.
- `Split.amount >= 0`; expense `amount > 0`.

> 💬 Noun extraction out loud: "The nouns are *user, expense, split, balance*. The verb 'split it three ways / by percent / by exact amount' is a **behavior that varies** — that's a Strategy, not data. So `Split` just holds the *result* (user + owed amount), and the *how* lives in a `SplitStrategy`."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are `User`, `Expense`, and `Split`. The one field that carries the whole design is `BalanceSheet._balances` — a nested map where `balances[A][B]` is the net amount B owes A, kept perfectly antisymmetric. Everything else exists to compute correct `Split` amounts and fold them into that map."

---

## 4. Class Design (diagram)

This is the canonical design — §3, §6, and §7 match it one-for-one.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class SplitType {
        <<enumeration>>
        EQUAL
        EXACT
        PERCENT
    }

    class User {
        +str user_id
        +str name
        +str email
    }

    class Split {
        +User user
        +Decimal amount
    }

    class Expense {
        +str expense_id
        +str description
        +Decimal amount
        +User paid_by
        +list~Split~ splits
        +SplitType split_type
    }

    class SplitStrategy {
        <<abstract>>
        +compute(amount, participants, values) list~Split~
    }
    class EqualSplitStrategy {
        +compute(...) list~Split~
    }
    class ExactSplitStrategy {
        +compute(...) list~Split~
    }
    class PercentSplitStrategy {
        +compute(...) list~Split~
    }

    class SplitStrategyFactory {
        -dict _registry
        +get(split_type) SplitStrategy
    }

    class BalanceSheet {
        -dict _balances
        +apply_expense(expense)
        +settle(payer, payee, amount)
        +balances_of(user_id) dict
    }

    class ExpenseManager {
        -dict _users
        -dict _expenses
        -BalanceSheet _sheet
        -RLock _lock
        +add_user(user)
        +add_expense(...) Expense
        +settle_up(payer_id, payee_id, amount)
        +get_balances(user_id) dict
    }

    SplitStrategy <|.. EqualSplitStrategy
    SplitStrategy <|.. ExactSplitStrategy
    SplitStrategy <|.. PercentSplitStrategy
    Expense "1" *-- "1..*" Split
    Expense --> User : paid_by
    Split --> User
    SplitStrategyFactory ..> SplitStrategy : creates
    ExpenseManager "1" *-- "1" BalanceSheet
    ExpenseManager o-- "0..*" User
    ExpenseManager o-- "0..*" Expense
    ExpenseManager ..> SplitStrategyFactory : uses
    ExpenseManager ..> SplitStrategy : uses
```

**Reading every relationship:**

- **Realization (dashed, `<|..`):** `EqualSplitStrategy`, `ExactSplitStrategy`, `PercentSplitStrategy` each **implement** the `SplitStrategy` contract (`compute`). This is the **extensibility seam** — the orchestrator depends only on the abstract `SplitStrategy`, so a new split type (e.g. "by shares") is a new class, zero changes to the manager.
- **Composition (filled diamond):** `Expense *-- Split` with multiplicity **1 → 1..\***. An `Expense` *owns* its `Split`s; kill the expense and its splits go with it — a `Split` has no meaning outside its expense.
- **Composition:** `ExpenseManager *-- BalanceSheet` (**1 → 1**). The manager owns exactly one balance sheet for its lifetime.
- **Association (`-->`):** `Expense --> User` (`paid_by`) and `Split --> User`. These are **references, not ownership** — users outlive any single expense. Multiplicity 1 each.
- **Aggregation (open diamond `o--`):** `ExpenseManager o-- User` (0..\*) and `o-- Expense` (0..\*). The manager holds registries of users and expenses by reference; they're aggregated, not lifetime-bound the way the balance sheet is.
- **Dependency (dashed `..>`):** `ExpenseManager ..> SplitStrategyFactory` and `..> SplitStrategy`, and `SplitStrategyFactory ..> SplitStrategy` (creates). Transient *uses* — the manager calls the factory to fetch a strategy, calls the strategy to compute, and keeps neither.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I put `ExpenseManager` in the middle — it's the facade. It owns one `BalanceSheet` (filled diamond, that's the truth) and registries of users and expenses. Off to the side is the `SplitStrategy` interface with three implementations — that's the plug-in point. When an expense comes in, the manager asks the factory for the right strategy, gets back a list of `Split`s, and folds them into the balance sheet. Notice the manager never knows *which* split type it's dealing with — it only talks to the interface."

---

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

**Patterns actually applied (visible in §6/§7 code):**

- **Strategy** — `SplitStrategy` with `Equal/Exact/Percent` implementations. The varying algorithm (how to divide a total) is encapsulated behind one interface.
  > 💬 Layman: "How you split the bill changes; *that* you split it doesn't. So I make 'how' a swappable part."
- **Factory** — `SplitStrategyFactory.get(split_type)` returns the right strategy. The manager doesn't `if/elif` over types.
- **Facade** — `ExpenseManager` gives clients one simple surface (`add_expense`, `settle_up`, `get_balances`) over users + strategies + balances. *(I call it Facade, not Singleton — I don't enforce a single instance.)*

**Extension point (not yet wired):** **Observer** for notifications (email/push when you're added to an expense) — clean to add as `BalanceSheet`/`ExpenseManager` subscribers, but I haven't wired it, so I won't claim it.

**SOLID:**
- **S** — `Split` holds data, `SplitStrategy` computes, `BalanceSheet` tracks debt, `ExpenseManager` orchestrates. One reason to change each.
- **O** — new split type = new strategy class; core untouched.
- **L** — every strategy is substitutable behind `compute`.
- **D** — manager depends on the `SplitStrategy` abstraction, not concretes.

Now let me build and verify it.

blocks.name: write_file

input.content:
"""Domain entities, enums, and exceptions for the Splitwise LLD."""
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from decimal import Decimal
from enum import Enum


class SplitType(Enum):
    """How an expense's total is divided among participants."""
    EQUAL = "EQUAL"
    EXACT = "EXACT"
    PERCENT = "PERCENT"


# --- Specific error types (never a bare Exception) ---------------------------
class SplitwiseError(Exception):
    """Base class for all domain errors."""


class UnknownUserError(SplitwiseError):
    """A referenced user id is not registered."""


class InvalidExpenseError(SplitwiseError):
    """Expense amount/participants/values are invalid or inconsistent."""


class InvalidSplitError(SplitwiseError):
    """Split values do not reconcile to the expense total."""


class DuplicateError(SplitwiseError):
    """An id collides with an existing entity."""


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class User:
    """An immutable participant identity (safe to share across threads)."""
    user_id: str
    name: str
    email: str


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Split:
    """One participant's owed share of an expense (a value object)."""
    user: User
    amount: Decimal


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Expense:
    """Immutable record of a payment and how it splits across participants."""
    expense_id: str
    description: str
    amount: Decimal
    paid_by: User
    splits: tuple[Split, ...]
    split_type: SplitType

input.content:
"""BalanceSheet (source of truth) + ExpenseManager (thread-safe facade)."""
from __future__ import annotations

import itertools
import threading
from collections import defaultdict
from decimal import Decimal

from models import (
    DuplicateError,
    Expense,
    InvalidExpenseError,
    SplitType,
    UnknownUserError,
    User,
)
from strategies import SplitStrategyFactory


class BalanceSheet:
    """Pairwise net debt. balances[A][B] = net amount B owes A.

    Invariant maintained on every mutation: balances[A][B] == -balances[B][A].
    """

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._balances: dict[str, dict[str, Decimal]] = defaultdict(
            lambda: defaultdict(lambda: Decimal("0"))
        )

    def _adjust(self, creditor: str, debtor: str, delta: Decimal) -> None:
        self._balances[creditor][debtor] += delta
        self._balances[debtor][creditor] -= delta

    def apply_expense(self, expense: Expense) -> None:
        payer = expense.paid_by.user_id
        for split in expense.splits:
            ower = split.user.user_id
            if ower == payer:
                continue  # payer's own share creates no debt
            # ower owes payer split.amount -> payer is creditor.
            self._adjust(payer, ower, split.amount)

    def settle(self, payer_id: str, payee_id: str, amount: Decimal) -> None:
        # payer pays payee back: reduces what payer owes payee.
        self._adjust(payer_id, payee_id, amount)

    def balances_of(self, user_id: str) -> dict[str, Decimal]:
        # Positive => the other user owes `user_id`; negative => user_id owes them.
        return {
            other: amt
            for other, amt in self._balances[user_id].items()
            if amt != Decimal("0")
        }


class ExpenseManager:
    """Facade orchestrating users, expenses, strategies, and balances.

    All shared mutable state (_users, _expenses, _sheet) is guarded by a single
    reentrant lock so check-then-act sequences are atomic.
    """

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._users: dict[str, User] = {}
        self._expenses: dict[str, Expense] = {}
        self._sheet = BalanceSheet()
        self._factory = SplitStrategyFactory()
        self._lock = threading.RLock()  # reentrant: guarded methods call helpers

    # --- registry --------------------------------------------------------
    def add_user(self, user: User) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if user.user_id in self._users:
                raise DuplicateError(f"user {user.user_id} already exists")
            self._users[user.user_id] = user

    def _require_user(self, user_id: str) -> User:
        user = self._users.get(user_id)
        if user is None:
            raise UnknownUserError(f"unknown user {user_id}")
        return user

    # --- core operations -------------------------------------------------
    def add_expense(
        self,
        expense_id: str,
        description: str,
        amount: Decimal,
        paid_by_id: str,
        participant_ids: list[str],
        split_type: SplitType,
        values: list[Decimal] | None = None,
    ) -> Expense:
        with self._lock:  # validate + mutate atomically
            if amount <= Decimal("0"):
                raise InvalidExpenseError("amount must be positive")
            if expense_id in self._expenses:
                raise DuplicateError(f"expense {expense_id} already exists")
            if not participant_ids:
                raise InvalidExpenseError("need at least one participant")

            payer = self._require_user(paid_by_id)
            participants = [self._require_user(pid) for pid in participant_ids]

            strategy = self._factory.get(split_type)
            splits = strategy.compute(amount, participants, values)

            expense = Expense(
                expense_id=expense_id,
                description=description,
                amount=amount,
                paid_by=payer,
                splits=tuple(splits),
                split_type=split_type,
            )
            self._sheet.apply_expense(expense)
            self._expenses[expense_id] = expense
            return expense

    def settle_up(self, payer_id: str, payee_id: str, amount: Decimal) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if amount <= Decimal("0"):
                raise InvalidExpenseError("settlement must be positive")
            self._require_user(payer_id)
            self._require_user(payee_id)
            self._sheet.settle(payer_id, payee_id, amount)

    def get_balances(self, user_id: str) -> dict[str, Decimal]:
        with self._lock:  # reads also lock: never observe a half-applied expense
            self._require_user(user_id)
            return self._sheet.balances_of(user_id)

    def simplify_group(self, user_ids: list[str]) -> list[tuple[str, str, Decimal]]:
        """Greedy debt simplification: minimal transfers to settle a group."""
        with self._lock:
            net: dict[str, Decimal] = {u: Decimal("0") for u in user_ids}
            for a, b in itertools.combinations(user_ids, 2):
                owed = self._sheet._balances[a][b]  # b owes a
                net[a] += owed
                net[b] -= owed
            creditors = [(u, v) for u, v in net.items() if v > 0]
            debtors = [(u, -v) for u, v in net.items() if v < 0]
            txns: list[tuple[str, str, Decimal]] = []
            i = j = 0
            while i < len(debtors) and j < len(creditors):
                d_user, d_amt = debtors[i]
                c_user, c_amt = creditors[j]
                pay = min(d_amt, c_amt)
                txns.append((d_user, c_user, pay))  # d_user pays c_user
                d_amt -= pay
                c_amt -= pay
                debtors[i] = (d_user, d_amt)
                creditors[j] = (c_user, c_amt)
                if d_amt == 0:
                    i += 1
                if c_amt == 0:
                    j += 1
            return txns

blocks.name: write_file

input.content:
"""Driver: exercises happy paths, boundary/invalid inputs, and concurrency."""
from __future__ import annotations

import threading
from decimal import Decimal

from manager import ExpenseManager
from models import (
    DuplicateError,
    InvalidExpenseError,
    InvalidSplitError,
    SplitType,
    UnknownUserError,
    User,
)

D = Decimal


def setup() -> ExpenseManager:
    m = ExpenseManager()
    for uid, name in [("u1", "Alice"), ("u2", "Bob"), ("u3", "Carol")]:
        m.add_user(User(uid, name, f"{name.lower()}@x.com"))
    return m


def test_equal_split_with_rounding():
    m = setup()
    # 100 / 3 -> 33.34, 33.33, 33.33 (drift goes to first); Alice paid.
    m.add_expense("e1", "Dinner", D("100"), "u1", ["u1", "u2", "u3"],
                  SplitType.EQUAL)
    assert m.get_balances("u1") == {"u2": D("33.33"), "u3": D("33.33")}
    assert m.get_balances("u2") == {"u1": D("-33.33")}
    print("equal+rounding OK:", m.get_balances("u1"))


def test_exact_and_percent():
    m = setup()
    m.add_expense("e1", "Cab", D("90"), "u2", ["u1", "u3"], SplitType.EXACT,
                  [D("40"), D("50")])
    assert m.get_balances("u2") == {"u1": D("40"), "u3": D("50")}

    m.add_expense("e2", "Hotel", D("300"), "u1", ["u1", "u2", "u3"],
                  SplitType.PERCENT, [D("50"), D("25"), D("25")])
    # Alice paid 300, owes herself 150 (ignored); Bob 75, Carol 75.
    assert m.get_balances("u1")["u2"] == D("75") + D("-40")  # netted with e1
    print("exact+percent OK:", m.get_balances("u1"))


def test_invalid_inputs():
    m = setup()
    # unknown user
    try:
        m.add_expense("e1", "x", D("10"), "ghost", ["u1"], SplitType.EQUAL)
        assert False
    except UnknownUserError:
        pass
    # negative amount
    try:
        m.add_expense("e2", "x", D("-5"), "u1", ["u2"], SplitType.EQUAL)
        assert False
    except InvalidExpenseError:
        pass
    # exact amounts that do not sum to total
    try:
        m.add_expense("e3", "x", D("100"), "u1", ["u2", "u3"],
                      SplitType.EXACT, [D("40"), D("40")])
        assert False
    except InvalidSplitError:
        pass
    # percentages not summing to 100
    try:
        m.add_expense("e4", "x", D("100"), "u1", ["u2", "u3"],
                      SplitType.PERCENT, [D("60"), D("30")])
        assert False
    except InvalidSplitError:
        pass
    # duplicate expense id
    m.add_expense("e5", "ok", D("10"), "u1", ["u2"], SplitType.EQUAL)
    try:
        m.add_expense("e5", "dup", D("10"), "u1", ["u2"], SplitType.EQUAL)
        assert False
    except DuplicateError:
        pass
    print("invalid-input rejection OK")


def test_settle_up():
    m = setup()
    m.add_expense("e1", "Lunch", D("60"), "u1", ["u1", "u2"], SplitType.EQUAL)
    assert m.get_balances("u2") == {"u1": D("-30")}  # Bob owes Alice 30
    m.settle_up("u2", "u1", D("30"))                  # Bob pays Alice back
    assert m.get_balances("u2") == {}                 # square
    print("settle-up OK")


def test_concurrency_no_lost_update():
    # 100 threads each add a 1.00 expense Bob owes Alice -> Alice owed exactly 100.
    m = setup()
    def worker(i):
        m.add_expense(f"c{i}", "x", D("1.00"), "u1", ["u2"], SplitType.EQUAL)
    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(i,)) for i in range(100)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()
    assert m.get_balances("u1") == {"u2": D("100.00")}, m.get_balances("u1")
    print("concurrency (no lost update) OK:", m.get_balances("u1"))


def test_simplify():
    m = setup()
    # Alice paid 30 split equally with Bob -> Bob owes Alice 15.
    m.add_expense("e1", "a", D("30"), "u1", ["u1", "u2"], SplitType.EQUAL)
    # Bob paid 30 split equally with Carol -> Carol owes Bob 15.
    m.add_expense("e2", "b", D("30"), "u2", ["u2", "u3"], SplitType.EQUAL)
    txns = m.simplify_group(["u1", "u2", "u3"])
    # Net: Bob is square, Carol owes Alice 15 -> single transfer.
    assert txns == [("u3", "u1", D("15"))], txns
    print("debt simplification OK:", txns)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_equal_split_with_rounding()
    test_exact_and_percent()
    test_invalid_inputs()
    test_settle_up()
    test_concurrency_no_lost_update()
    test_simplify()
    print("\nALL TESTS PASSED")

blocks.md:
All green. Now the narrated walkthrough.

---

## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class

**`models.py` — entities, enums, errors.** Everything here is a frozen (immutable) value object, so `User`/`Split`/`Expense` are safe to share across threads without copying. I define specific exceptions rather than raising bare `Exception`.

```python
class SplitType(Enum):
    EQUAL = "EQUAL"; EXACT = "EXACT"; PERCENT = "PERCENT"

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class User:
    user_id: str; name: str; email: str

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Split:
    user: User; amount: Decimal

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Expense:
    expense_id: str; description: str; amount: Decimal
    paid_by: User; splits: tuple[Split, ...]; split_type: SplitType
```
> 💬 "I make these immutable on purpose — an `Expense` is a historical fact. Once recorded it never mutates, which also means it's trivially thread-safe."

**`strategies.py` — the Strategy pattern.** `SplitStrategy.compute` is the seam. Each implementation's only job is to return splits that **sum exactly to the total**; the tricky part is rounding.

```python
class EqualSplitStrategy(SplitStrategy):
    def compute(self, amount, participants, values=None) -> list[Split]:
        n = len(participants)
        if n == 0:
            raise InvalidSplitError("equal split needs at least one participant")
        base = _round(amount / n)
        splits = [Split(u, base) for u in participants]
        drift = amount - base * n          # e.g. 100 - 33.33*3 = 0.01
        if drift != Decimal("0"):
            first = splits[0]
            splits[0] = Split(first.user, _round(first.amount + drift))
        return splits
```
> 💬 Tricky method: "100 split 3 ways is 33.333… Banks can't bill a third of a cent, so I round each to 33.33 and push the leftover cent onto the first person. The shares now sum to exactly 100 — that invariant is what keeps balances from leaking fractions of a cent over thousands of expenses."

`ExactSplitStrategy` rejects values that don't sum to the total; `PercentSplitStrategy` rejects percentages that don't sum to 100, then applies the same drift fix. The **factory** maps a `SplitType` to a (stateless, reusable) strategy:

```python
class SplitStrategyFactory:
    def __init__(self):
        self._registry = {SplitType.EQUAL: EqualSplitStrategy(),
                          SplitType.EXACT: ExactSplitStrategy(),
                          SplitType.PERCENT: PercentSplitStrategy()}
    def get(self, split_type): return self._registry[split_type]
```

**`manager.py` — `BalanceSheet` (the truth).** The whole design hinges on `_adjust`, which keeps the antisymmetry invariant by touching both directions in one step:

```python
def _adjust(self, creditor, debtor, delta):
    self._balances[creditor][debtor] += delta
    self._balances[debtor][creditor] -= delta
```
> 💬 "`balances[A][B]` is what B owes A. Any change updates both `[A][B]` and `[B][A]` together, so the sheet is never internally inconsistent. A settlement is just a `_adjust` in the opposite direction — same code path."

**`ExpenseManager` — the thread-safe facade.** `add_expense` validates **and** mutates inside one `RLock`, so the check-then-act is atomic:

```python
def add_expense(self, expense_id, description, amount, paid_by_id,
                participant_ids, split_type, values=None):
    with self._lock:
        if amount <= Decimal("0"): raise InvalidExpenseError("amount must be positive")
        if expense_id in self._expenses: raise DuplicateError(...)
        payer = self._require_user(paid_by_id)
        participants = [self._require_user(p) for p in participant_ids]
        splits = self._factory.get(split_type).compute(amount, participants, values)
        expense = Expense(expense_id, description, amount, payer, tuple(splits), split_type)
        self._sheet.apply_expense(expense)
        self._expenses[expense_id] = expense
        return expense
```
> 💬 "Notice the manager never branches on split type — it asks the factory for a strategy and trusts the interface. Adding 'split by shares' tomorrow doesn't touch this method."

---

## 7. Putting It Together (verified run)

The driver in `main.py` exercises rounding, exact+percent, every invalid path, settlement, a 100-thread concurrency race, and debt simplification. **Actual output:**

```
equal+rounding OK: {'u2': Decimal('33.33'), 'u3': Decimal('33.33')}
exact+percent OK: {'u2': Decimal('35.00'), 'u3': Decimal('75.00')}
invalid-input rejection OK
settle-up OK
concurrency (no lost update) OK: {'u2': Decimal('100.00')}
debt simplification OK: [('u3', 'u1', Decimal('15.00'))]

ALL TESTS PASSED
```

The concurrency test is the key proof: 100 threads each add a $1 expense Bob owes Alice; the final balance is **exactly $100.00** — no lost updates.

---

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

One `add_expense` call, end to end:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Client
    participant M as ExpenseManager
    participant F as SplitStrategyFactory
    participant S as SplitStrategy
    participant B as BalanceSheet

    Client->>M: add_expense(amount, paidBy, parts, type, values)
    activate M
    Note over M: acquire RLock - validate inside
    M->>M: validate amount, dup id, users
    M->>F: get(split_type)
    F-->>M: strategy
    M->>S: compute(amount, participants, values)
    S-->>M: list of Split (sums to total)
    M->>B: apply_expense(expense)
    B->>B: _adjust(payer, ower, share) per split
    M-->>Client: Expense
    deactivate M
```

**Narration:** the manager locks, validates (amount, duplicate id, all users known), asks the factory for the matching strategy, gets back a reconciled list of splits, and folds each into the balance sheet — every step inside the same critical section so no other thread sees a half-applied expense.

---

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**Concurrency & thread-safety model.** Shared mutable state is exactly three things: `_users`, `_expenses`, and the `_sheet`. All are guarded by **one reentrant lock** on `ExpenseManager`. Reentrant because `add_expense` (holding the lock) calls `_require_user` and `_sheet.apply_expense`, which I want to keep simple/lockable without self-deadlock.
- **Races eliminated:** *find-then-assign* style hazards — duplicate-id check then insert, and read-net-then-update balances — are inside one `with self._lock`, so check-then-act is atomic.
- **Reads also lock** (`get_balances`, `simplify_group`), so a query never observes a balance mid-`_adjust` (which writes two cells).
- **Granularity:** one coarse lock. Trade-off: simple and correct, but serializes all writes. At scale I'd shard by group or move balances to a row-per-pair in a DB and use `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` or a compare-and-set on a version column instead of a process lock.
- **Proof:** the 100-thread test returns exactly `$100.00` — no lost updates.

**Other correctness points:** value objects are **immutable** so they're freely shareable; settlement is the same `_adjust` path so it composes cleanly; the only lock means no lock-ordering/deadlock concern today (when I add per-group locks I'd acquire them in sorted id order).

**Edge cases handled (each exercised in §7):**

| Edge case | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Negative/zero amount | `InvalidExpenseError` |
| Unknown user id | `UnknownUserError` |
| Exact amounts ≠ total | `InvalidSplitError` |
| Percentages ≠ 100 | `InvalidSplitError` |
| Duplicate expense id | `DuplicateError` |
| Rounding remainder (100/3) | drift pushed to first split; shares sum to total |
| Payer is also a participant | own share creates no self-debt |
| Full settlement | balance returns to empty `{}` |

**Extensibility without rewrites:**

| Want to add | How | What it touches |
|---|---|---|
| New split type (by shares) | New `SplitStrategy` subclass + register | `strategies.py` only |
| Notifications | Observer subscribing to `apply_expense` | new file; manager emits event |
| Multi-currency | Add `currency` to `Expense`, convert in strategy | `models.py`, strategies |
| Persistence | Back `BalanceSheet` with a DB repo | `manager.py` only |

**Hardest follow-ups & where the design answers them:**
- *"Two threads add expenses touching the same pair — lost update?"* → single `RLock`; `_adjust` is atomic; 100-thread test proves it.
- *"Fractions of a cent over millions of expenses?"* → `Decimal` + drift redistribution keeps every expense's splits summing exactly to the total.
- *"Add a new split type without redeploying the core?"* → Strategy + Factory; new class, register it, manager untouched.
- *"Minimize transactions to settle a group?"* → `simplify_group` greedy net-off; verified to collapse a 3-person chain to one transfer.
- *"Distribute across processes?"* → move the coarse lock to DB row locks / CAS; the `BalanceSheet` interface is the seam.

> 🎙️ **Concurrency script:** "All shared state lives behind one reentrant lock on the manager. Every write — and every read — takes it, so check-then-act sequences like dup-detection and balance updates are atomic, and a query never sees a half-applied expense. I proved it with a hundred threads racing to add expenses for the same pair; the balance lands on exactly the right number. To scale past one process I'd swap the in-memory lock for per-row DB locking, and nothing above the BalanceSheet changes."

> 💬 **30-second summary:** "Splitwise is three ideas: immutable `Expense`/`Split` records, a `SplitStrategy` interface (equal/exact/percent) resolved by a factory so the core never branches on type, and a `BalanceSheet` of pairwise net debts kept antisymmetric — `balances[A][B] == -balances[B][A]`. The `ExpenseManager` facade validates and mutates under one reentrant lock so it's thread-safe. Strategies guarantee splits sum exactly to the total with cent-level rounding, and a greedy `simplify_group` minimizes settle-up transactions. New behavior drops in as a new strategy — the core is closed for modification, open for extension."
